skill.name: kaizen
skill.description: Guide for continuous improvement, error proofing, and standardization. Use this skill when the user wants to improve code quality, refactor, or discuss process improvements.
skill.summary: Kaizen methodology for software development: small improvements, error-proofing, standardized patterns, and just-in-time implementation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation-only guide for Kaizen software development methodology. Static analysis flagged 73 patterns in code examples within markdown documentation, but all findings are false positives. The backtick operators are TypeScript template literals (not shell commands), fetch calls are educational examples (not actual network requests), and environment variable references teach secure validation practices. No executable code exists in this skill.

low_findings.title: External Command Pattern (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected 44 instances of backtick operators. These are TypeScript template literals used for string interpolation in code examples (e.g., `/users/${id}`), not Ruby/shell command execution. The skill contains documentation only with no executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All backtick occurrences are within markdown code blocks showing TypeScript examples. Template literal syntax matches the backtick pattern but serves string interpolation, not shell execution.

low_findings.title: Network Pattern (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected fetch API calls and hardcoded URLs. These are educational code examples demonstrating proper API client patterns. The URLs are example placeholders (api.example.com), not actual external endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Fetch calls appear only within documentation code blocks illustrating standardized work patterns. No actual network requests are made by this skill.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Pattern (False Positive)
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected environment variable access and potential secret exposure. Line 304-306 shows educational content teaching secure config validation - specifically demonstrating how to throw errors when API_KEY is missing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code example explicitly teaches security best practices by validating required environment variables and failing fast with clear error messages.

content.user_title: Apply Kaizen Methods for Code Quality
content.value_statement: Software teams struggle with inconsistent code quality and reactive error handling. This skill provides a structured Kaizen methodology for continuous improvement, error-proofing designs, and following standardized patterns.
actual_capabilities: Guide incremental code improvements following Kaizen principles
Apply Poka-Yoke error-proofing techniques to prevent bugs at design time
Enforce standardized coding patterns across the codebase
Implement just-in-time development to avoid over-engineering
Provide structured problem analysis using root cause methods
Support iterative refactoring with verification at each step
limitations: Focuses on process and methodology rather than domain-specific solutions
Requires team agreement on patterns for standardized work to be effective
Incremental approach may not suit urgent situations requiring immediate large changes
Does not provide automated tooling or linting rules directly

use_cases.title: Code Review Improvement
use_cases.description: Apply Kaizen principles during code reviews to suggest small, incremental improvements rather than demanding complete rewrites. Each suggestion is verified and tested before moving to the next.
use_cases.target_user: Senior developers and tech leads conducting code reviews

use_cases.title: Error-Resistant API Design
use_cases.description: Use Poka-Yoke techniques to design TypeScript APIs where invalid states are unrepresentable at compile time. Type system catches errors before runtime.

use_cases.title: Refactoring Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Apply iterative refinement approach: make it work, make it clear, make it efficient. Each iteration is complete and tested before proceeding to the next.
use_cases.target_user: Developers maintaining and modernizing legacy codebases

prompt_templates.title: Basic Code Improvement
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this function and suggest one small improvement following Kaizen principles. Explain why this change improves quality and verify it works before suggesting anything else.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick iterative improvement during active development

prompt_templates.title: Error-Proof Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this API interface using Poka-Yoke principles. Show how to use TypeScript types to make invalid states unrepresentable. Provide before and after examples with type definitions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Designing new APIs or refactoring existing ones for safety

prompt_templates.title: Pattern Standardization
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm adding a new feature to this codebase. Analyze the existing code patterns for [feature type] and show me how to implement the new feature following the same standardized approach. Point to specific examples in the codebase.
prompt_templates.scenario: Maintaining consistency when adding new functionality

prompt_templates.title: YAGNI Refactoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code for YAGNI violations. Identify any 'just in case' features, premature abstractions, or unused complexity. For each issue, show the simplest version that meets current requirements and explain what to delete.
prompt_templates.scenario: Cleaning up over-engineered code and reducing complexity

output_examples.input: Apply Kaizen to improve this function that calculates order totals
output: Iteration 1 - Add input validation: Check for null/empty items array and negative prices before calculation. This prevents runtime errors.
Iteration 2 - Improve readability: Replace for-loop with reduce() for clearer intent. Type signature explicitly returns number.
Iteration 3 - Add error messages: Throw descriptive errors for invalid inputs instead of silent failures. Each iteration is tested and working.

output_examples.input: Design an error-proof Order type using Poka-Yoke
output: Instead of a simple object with string status, use discriminated union types where each order state has its required data.
Pending orders require createdAt timestamp. Shipped orders require trackingNumber. Delivered orders require signature.
The type system makes it impossible to have a shipped order without tracking information. Compile errors prevent entire classes of bugs.

best_practices: Make the smallest viable change that improves quality, verify it works, then proceed to the next improvement
Validate inputs and configuration at system boundaries, fail fast with clear error messages
Follow existing codebase patterns for consistency. Only introduce new patterns when significantly better and agreed by team
anti_patterns: Attempting large refactoring projects instead of incremental improvements
Building generic frameworks or abstractions before having multiple proven use cases
Adding 'just in case' features or optimizing without measuring actual performance issues

faq.question: When should I use Kaizen versus doing a complete rewrite?
faq.answer: Kaizen works for most improvements. Rewrites are rarely the answer. Break large problems into small, testable improvements. Each iteration should leave the code working and better than before.

faq.question: How do I apply Poka-Yoke in dynamically typed languages?
faq.answer: Use runtime validation at boundaries, assertion functions, and branded types. While not as strong as compile-time checking, early validation with clear errors still prevents many production issues.

faq.question: What if my team has no established patterns?
faq.answer: Start by documenting patterns you observe in working code. Propose standards based on existing successful patterns. Use linters and type checking to automate enforcement once agreed.

faq.question: How do I know if an abstraction is premature?
faq.answer: Wait for the Rule of Three: implement the same pattern three times in different places, then abstract. Duplication is cheaper than wrong abstraction. Abstraction should emerge from proven patterns.

faq.question: Can Kaizen work with tight deadlines?
faq.answer: Yes. Small improvements are faster than large rewrites. Focus on highest-impact changes first. The iterative approach reduces risk of broken code under deadline pressure.

faq.question: What tools support Kaizen methodology?
faq.answer: TypeScript for compile-time error prevention, ESLint/Prettier for standardized formatting, testing frameworks for verification at each iteration, and CI/CD for automated quality gates.
